title: What Lies Between Crystal and Randomly Packed Structures? A General Characterization of Non-Periodic Order
abstract: In this paper we address the characterization of the structure of condensed materials, periodic and non-periodic. Carrying out an extensive study of over 7000 different groundstate structures of a 2D lattice model of binary packing, we find a predominance of non-periodic structures (over 96%) that extend across the entire range of possible diversities. These non-periodic structures are resolved by establishing whether a structure will accommodate or reject additional local structures. This property, structural selectivity, is treated as a signature of an underlying ordering principle. The major result of the paper is the determination that roughly 35% of the non-periodic structures are selective and, hence, ordered in some way. This selectivity extends up to a diversity of ~ 9, well beyond the upper threshold for diversity in periodically ordered states.
summary: This paper provides a general framework for identifying order in non-periodic materials by using 'structural selectivity' as a metric. By studying thousands of groundstates in a 2D lattice model, the authors demonstrate that a significant portion of non-periodic structures are not merely random but follow identifiable ordering principles.
main_problem: The paper seeks to characterize the structural landscape between periodic crystals and random/amorphous solids, specifically investigating if non-periodic structures can be classified by underlying ordering principles.
main_result: The study finds that 96% of groundstate structures are non-periodic, and approximately 35% of these exhibit 'structural selectivity,' a signature of order that persists even at high structural diversity.
method: The authors performed extensive simulations using simulated annealing (cooling) to identify groundstate structures for thousands of different Hamiltonian configurations.
model_or_system: A 2D Favored Local Structure (FLS) model on a triangular lattice where each site is occupied by an A or B particle, and a many-body Hamiltonian assigns energy -1 to a user-selected set of 'favored' local structures.
key_observables: Structural diversity (S1, using Hill's numbers), structural selectivity, and computable information density (CID).
important_parameters: Structural diversity (S1), unit cell size (Z), and the number of distinct local structures (14).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on groundstate structures to avoid thermal fluctuation complications; the results are based on a 2D lattice model which may not fully capture 3D off-lattice complexities.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the 14 possible local structures; Figures 7-8 show distributions of diversity for selective vs. non-selective structures; Figure 9 provides visual examples of different non-periodic selective categories; Figure 10 shows examples of disordered structures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of characterizing non-periodic order, describes the FLS model and simulation protocol, presents the results regarding the prevalence of non-periodicity and selectivity, classifies types of non-periodic order, and discusses limitations and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
